title: Building Networks: Layers, Activations, and the Forward Pass
learningObjectives: Define a neural network as a subclass of nn.Module with a forward() method
Explain what a linear layer actually computes and why stacking them alone isn't enough
Explain why a nonlinear activation function (ReLU, etc.) is required between layers
Pass a batch of feature data through a network and interpret the raw output shape
whyItMatters: Autograd gives you gradients for any computation; a neural network is a specific, structured computation — layers of weighted sums and nonlinearities — designed to be flexible enough to learn complex patterns your scikit-learn linear model architecturally couldn't represent.
capstoneContribution: The actual architecture of the Digital Twin's first neural forecaster — the specific arrangement of layers and activations that gives it more expressive power than the linear regression baseline from Mission 3.

problem.summary: A linear model architecturally can't represent curved, interacting relationships.
problem.content: Your Mission 3 linear regression could only ever learn a straight-line relationship between features and demand. Real demand almost certainly has curved, interacting relationships (e.g. the effect of temperature on demand differs at different times of day) that a purely linear model architecturally cannot represent, no matter how well it's trained.

motivation.summary: Neural networks exist to represent a much richer space of functions, given the right structure.
motivation.content: This is the actual reason neural networks exist: not to be more 'AI,' but to represent a much richer space of functions than a linear model can, provided you give them the right structure and enough data to learn it from.

theory.summary: Linear layers alone collapse into one line; nonlinear activations are what add real expressive power.
theory.content: `nn.Linear(in_features, out_features)` computes a weighted sum plus bias, exactly like linear regression, for each output. Stacking multiple `nn.Linear` layers directly, with nothing between them, mathematically collapses back into a single linear layer — no added expressive power. A nonlinear activation function (`nn.ReLU()`, applied between linear layers) is what actually lets a stack of layers represent curved, interacting relationships. A network is defined as a class inheriting from `nn.Module`, with layers created in `__init__` and the actual computation defined in `forward()`, which PyTorch calls automatically when you call the model like a function.

visualExplanation.summary: Two stacked linear layers flatten to one line; adding ReLU lets the fit bend.
visualExplanation.content: Two linear layers stacked directly (mathematically flattening to one line) sit next to the same two layers with a ReLU activation in between (now able to bend), visualized as fitting a curved dataset.

interactiveDemo.summary: Toggle ReLU on/off between layers and watch the fitted curve flatten or bend.
interactiveDemo.content: Toggle a ReLU activation on and off between two linear layers on a live curve-fitting visualization, watching the model's fitted curve flatten into a straight line or bend to match curved data accordingly.

guidedCoding.summary: Follow along: define a small network as an nn.Module and run a forward pass.
guidedCoding.content:
```python
import torch.nn as nn

class DemandForecaster(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, n_features):
        super().__init__()
        self.layer1 = nn.Linear(n_features, 16)
        self.relu = nn.ReLU()
        self.layer2 = nn.Linear(16, 1)

    def forward(self, x):
        x = self.layer1(x)
        x = self.relu(x)
        return self.layer2(x)

model = DemandForecaster(n_features=3)
output = model(sample_batch)
print(output.shape)
```

independentCoding.summary: Define a deeper network for the overload-classification task and check its output shape.
independentCoding.content: Define a network with three linear layers and two ReLU activations for the overload-classification task from Mission 3, and pass a sample batch through it to confirm the output shape matches what a binary classifier needs.

debugging.summary: Fix a network with no activation between two linear layers.
debugging.content: A network stacking two `nn.Linear` layers with no activation between them trains but never outperforms the plain linear regression baseline no matter how it's tuned. Diagnose why and fix by adding the missing nonlinearity.

reflection.summary: A network with no activation can never beat a single linear regression.
reflection.content: Why would a two-layer network with no activation function between the layers never be able to outperform a single linear regression, even with perfect training?

teachBack.summary: Explain why stacking linear layers alone doesn't add expressive power.
teachBack.content: Explain to someone why stacking linear layers alone doesn't add expressive power, but adding a ReLU between them does, using the flattening-to-one-line observation as your proof.

miniProject.summary: Define the actual neural demand forecaster architecture.
miniProject.content: Define the actual architecture for the Digital Twin's neural demand forecaster: an `nn.Module` with at least two hidden layers and ReLU activations, sized appropriately for your feature table's input dimensions.

portfolioEvidence.summary: This is the network the rest of this mission will train, tune, and evaluate.
portfolioEvidence.content: This architecture definition is the actual neural network the rest of this mission will train, tune, and evaluate against the Mission 3 baseline.

labs.title: Architecture Definition
labs.description: Sandboxed exercise defining an nn.Module network with correct layer sizing and activation placement, with a planted no-activation network to diagnose.

simulations.title: Activation Curve-Fitting Visualizer
simulations.description: Live curve-fitting demo toggling a ReLU activation between two linear layers, showing the fitted function flatten or bend accordingly.

flashcards.front: Why does stacking two nn.Linear layers directly, with nothing between them, add no expressive power over a single linear layer?
flashcards.back: Mathematically, a linear function of a linear function is still just a linear function — the two layers collapse into an equivalent single linear transformation, with no added ability to represent curves or interactions.

flashcards.front: What is forward() responsible for in an nn.Module subclass?
flashcards.back: It defines the actual computation the network performs on an input — how the layers are applied in sequence — and PyTorch calls it automatically when you call the model instance like a function.

rubric: Correctly diagnoses why a no-activation two-layer network can't outperform linear regression
Defined network architecture correctly places activations between, not after, every linear layer
Output shape from the forward pass matches what the downstream task (regression or classification) actually requires
